questions.question: Define deadlock and explain why it is considered a serious problem in operating systems.
keyPoints: Deadlock is permanent blocking where processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other
It cannot be resolved without external intervention
All four Coffman conditions must be present
Brings computation to a halt in affected processes

questions.question: Explain the 'Hold and Wait' condition for deadlock with an example.
keyPoints: Process holds at least one resource while waiting for additional resources
Can lead to resource starvation for other processes
Example: Process holding printer waits for scanner, while process holding scanner waits for printer
Prevents resources from being freely available

questions.question: What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)? Explain its components.
keyPoints: Directed graph representing system state
Two types of nodes: process nodes (circles) and resource type nodes (squares)
Two types of edges: assignment edge (process→resource) and request edge (resource→process)
Used to detect potential deadlock situations

questions.question: Differentiate between deadlock and starvation.
keyPoints: Deadlock: permanent blocking, processes wait indefinitely
Starvation: indefinite postponement, may eventually get resource
Deadlock requires circular wait, starvation does not
Deadlock needs all four Coffman conditions

questions.question: Explain the 'No Preemption' condition in deadlock characterization.
keyPoints: Resources cannot be forcibly taken away from processes
Once allocated, resources can only be released voluntarily
Applies to many OS resources like locks, files, memory
Violating this condition is often not feasible for certain resource types

questions.question: What is circular wait? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Circular wait exists when processes form a waiting chain
P1 waits for P2, P2 waits for P3, ..., Pn waits for P1
Prevention: impose total ordering of resource types
Processes must request resources in ascending order only

questions.question: Explain Mutual Exclusion as a necessary condition for deadlock.
keyPoints: Only one process can use a resource at a time
Some resources are inherently non-shareable (printers, databases)
Not all resources cause deadlock - depends on resource type
Without mutual exclusion, resources could be shared freely

questions.question: Explain in detail the four necessary conditions (Coffman conditions) for deadlock with appropriate examples for each condition.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can use resource at a time - example: single printer
Hold and Wait: Process holds resources while waiting for others - example: P1 holds R1, waits for R2
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken - example: locks cannot be preempted
Circular Wait: Circular chain of waiting processes - example: P1→P2→P1
All four must exist simultaneously for deadlock
Coffman conditions are necessary but not sufficient - all must hold

questions.question: Draw and explain the Resource Allocation Graph method for deadlock characterization. How can you determine if deadlock exists from the RAG?
keyPoints: Components: Process nodes (circles), Resource type nodes (squares with dots representing instances)
Assignment edge: process→resource (resource allocated)
Request edge: resource→process (process waiting)
Cycle detection: if cycle exists with single-instance resources → deadlock guaranteed
With multiple instances: cycle may or may not mean deadlock
RAG reduction: sequentially remove non-cyclic edges to check for remaining cycles

questions.question: Describe the system model for deadlock. How does the allocation matrix help in deadlock characterization?
keyPoints: System consists of processes P = {P1, P2, ..., Pn} and resource types R = {R1, R2, ..., Rm}
Each resource type Ri has 'Ci' number of instances
Allocation matrix shows which process holds which resource
Request matrix shows which resources each process needs
Max matrix shows maximum demand of each process
These matrices help determine system state and potential deadlock

questions.question: Consider a system with 3 processes P1, P2, P3 and 3 resource types R1, R2, R3 with 1 instance each. P1 holds R1 and requests R2, P2 holds R2 and requests R3, P3 holds R3 and requests R1. Using RAG, analyze whether deadlock exists and explain your reasoning.
keyPoints: Draw RAG: Assignment edges P1→R1, P2→R2, P3→R3
Request edges R2→P1, R3→P2, R1→P3
This forms a cycle: P1→R1→P3→R3→P2→R2→P1
Each resource type has exactly 1 instance
With single-instance resources in a cycle, deadlock is guaranteed
All four Coffman conditions are satisfied
